skill.name: google-image-api-skill
skill.description: Extract structured Google Images result metadata through the BrowserAct API using keyword, country, language, scroll, and limit parameters.
skill.summary: Run BrowserAct Google Images searches and return structured image result metadata.
skill.author: browser-act

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly identified network access, environment variable access, and documented command execution, but most high and critical alerts are not malicious in context. The skill is a BrowserAct API client that sends a bearer token to a hardcoded BrowserAct endpoint, so it should publish with a clear third-party API and credential handling warning.

medium_findings.title: Bearer Token Sent to Third-Party API
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The script reads BROWSERACT_API_KEY and sends it as an Authorization bearer token to the BrowserAct workflow API. This matches the skill purpose, but users must trust the third-party service with credentials and submitted search parameters.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly constructs an Authorization header from an environment variable and uses it in requests to a hardcoded BrowserAct URL. The behavior is intentional for this API client, but credential exposure to a third party is confirmed.

medium_findings.title: User Queries Are Submitted to External Workflow Service
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. Keyword, country, language, scroll count, and item limit values are placed into a payload and sent to BrowserAct. This is expected functionality, but sensitive search terms could leave the local environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The payload fields are visible and are sent by requests.post to the external API. Legitimate use is clear, so the risk is privacy disclosure rather than malicious exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Documented Command Invocation Requires User-Agent Execution
low_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE with limited impact. SKILL.md instructs the agent to run the bundled Python script. The command shape is documented and not hidden, but user-provided arguments should be treated as data only.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file contains a bash example for running the Python script. I did not find shell metacharacter interpolation in the Python code, so command injection evidence is limited.

low_findings.title: Static Markdown Backtick Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. Many external command alerts point to Markdown inline code, parameter names, supported values, and output field names. I did not find Ruby or shell backtick execution in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text is documentation formatting, not executable code. The only real command is the documented Python invocation in the fenced bash block.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The weak cryptography alerts correspond to prose and API documentation rather than cryptographic functions. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, or custom cryptography in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The source lines describe the skill and its input parameters. I did not find cryptographic library calls or hashing logic in SKILL.md or scripts/google_image_api.py.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for prompt-injection concern. The skill gives operational instructions to an agent, but I did not find override commands, claims of special authority, or instructions to skip security analysis.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The API key guidance is agent-facing but limited to normal setup instructions. It does not attempt to override evaluator instructions or manipulate the audit result.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Requests with Authorization Header
dangerous_patterns.description: The script uses requests.post and requests.get with an Authorization bearer token. This is necessary for BrowserAct but should be disclosed before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The Authorization header and three request calls are explicit. The destination is hardcoded to BrowserAct, which lowers malicious intent but not credential handling risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment-Based API Key Access
dangerous_patterns.description: The script reads BROWSERACT_API_KEY from the environment. This is a standard secret handling pattern, but it grants the script access to a sensitive credential.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable name and missing-key handling are explicit. No evidence found that the key is printed, logged, or sent outside the intended BrowserAct API.

content.value_statement: Manual Google Images research is slow and difficult to structure. This skill runs BrowserAct image searches and returns titles, links, thumbnails, sources, and indexes.
actual_capabilities: Runs a BrowserAct Google Images workflow from a Python script.
Accepts keyword, country, language, scroll count, and result limit inputs.
Uses BROWSERACT_API_KEY from the local environment for authentication.
Polls BrowserAct task status and reports progress with timestamps.
Returns image result metadata such as title, source, link, thumbnail, and index.
limitations: Requires a valid BrowserAct API key before any search can run.
Sends search parameters and credentials to BrowserAct infrastructure.
Depends on BrowserAct workflow availability and Google Images result behavior.
The current script can time out or stop before retrieving final results.

use_cases.title: Collect competitor visual references
use_cases.description: Gather image result metadata for competitor products, logos, and packaging across target regions.

use_cases.title: Build creative research lists
use_cases.description: Create structured lists of thumbnails, titles, and sources for campaign or mood board exploration.

use_cases.title: Compare localized image results
use_cases.description: Search the same topic in different countries and languages to compare regional visual trends.

prompt_templates.title: Basic image search
prompt_templates.prompt: Find Google Images metadata for "modern office chair" using the default country, language, and result settings.

prompt_templates.title: Localized product research
prompt_templates.prompt: Search Google Images for "running shoes" in Japan with Japanese language results. Return titles, sources, links, and thumbnails.

prompt_templates.title: Controlled result collection
prompt_templates.scenario: Limit scope for a research task
prompt_templates.prompt: Collect up to 30 Google Images results for "electric cargo bike" in Germany. Use three scroll actions and summarize source patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-market comparison
prompt_templates.prompt: Run separate Google Images searches for "skin care packaging" in the United States, France, and Korea. Compare recurring brands, source sites, and thumbnail themes.

output_examples.input: Search for "minimalist desk lamp" in the United States.
output: A structured list of image titles, source sites, click-through links, thumbnail URLs, and result indexes.
A short note identifying repeated retailers or design publications in the result set.

output_examples.input: Compare "winter jacket" image results in Canada and Japan.
output: Two localized result summaries with source names, thumbnails, and titles.
A comparison of visible product styles, recurring marketplaces, and language differences.

output_examples.input: Collect product-style images for "wireless earbuds" with a limit of 25.
output_examples.output: The response contains up to 25 image metadata records and flags product-style listings when available.

best_practices: Use non-sensitive search terms because queries are sent to BrowserAct.
Set conservative result limits before scaling collection jobs.
Verify source links and usage rights before reusing any image.
anti_patterns: Do not paste API keys into prompts or command arguments.
Do not use this skill for confidential investigations without approval.
Do not assume thumbnails or source links grant image reuse rights.

faq.question: Does this skill require an API key?
faq.answer: Yes. It requires BROWSERACT_API_KEY in the environment before the script can run.

faq.question: What data does it return?
faq.answer: It can return titles, click-through links, thumbnails, source names, source logos, product flags, related content IDs, and indexes.

faq.question: Does it download full image files?
faq.answer: No. The documented output is result metadata and thumbnail URLs, not full image downloads.

faq.question: Can it search by country and language?
faq.answer: Yes. The script accepts country and language parameters supported by the skill documentation.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: It is publishable with warnings because it uses a third-party API and environment-based credentials.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, or Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
